segments.text: You might have to come up.
Chin it camera. We going drop him too.
He's next. Yeah. You the main meal. He
With a On can raise the first.
Hello. All right. Good morning.
Thank you very much for being along here with us today.
Uh, we have 30 minutes set down for the briefing in relation
to the International Treaty Agreement on climate change,
trade and sustainability.
Um, we'll just say
that it is normal practice in the committee
that we would receive a written briefing
before hearing, uh, from you on this.
And so we are disappointed that we don't have that to refer
to in this instance.
Um, but we obviously will hand over to you for
your verbal briefing and then questions.
My apologies for that. Um,
and we can certainly provide a, uh,
a written briefing, uh, as well.
Sure. Including in terms of the National Impact Assessment,
which received also.
Okay. Over to you. So
Chair, thank you very much. And again,
I do apologize for that.
Um, on the, the written piece, um, just to
Give you a short introduction to, um, this agreement.
So what I talk about is briefly some of the its origins,
but explain what the concept is
because this is a, a small membership agreement.
It's only for, but it is something
that we call an open PLU lateral agreement,
and it's designed to expand over time.
And I wanna explain a little bit about
experience working on that.
And then very specifically,
I wanna speak about the agreement in three parts.
One, the classical Orthodox trade response to,
uh, environmental questions.
So environmental goods and services.
Uh, then I wanna talk about the, um,
fossil fuel subsidy disciplines
and the eco labeling elements.
And then, um, conclude in, which is a,
perhaps the first question is why do this agreement?
And there's three reasons. Uh, the first is, um,
New Zealand has a long history of trying to
take out distortions in the international trading system.
Agricultural subsidies are the classical piece
that we've been working on for as long as I can remember,
and more than 50 years of work in the World Trade
Organization on, uh, on that.
Um, ag and agricultural fishery subsidies have been our
primary focus, uh, but we have now expanded
because we do see these new distortions in the system.
And that brings me to the second part, which is
what are these distortions?
They're distortions in terms of the advantage
that our competitors have, um,
because they are able, um,
to produce products at a lower price
because they're being subsidized by their governments
through the inputs
that they get from the fossil fuel subsidies,
whether it's a production subsidy or a consumption subsidy.
Either way, these are a very significant, uh,
distortion into the system
and certainly does not, uh, help us in terms
of the competitiveness that we feel out there.
So lowest prices lowers, uh, returns
to New Zealand exporters,
not just agriculture, but across the board.
The third reason is, um, a strong view on our side that, uh,
trade is one of the tools
that we can address climate change.
It's not silver bullet, but certainly, uh, trade
and trade agreements need to be able to make a contribution.
And that's one of the, uh,
primary reasons why we wanted to do this.
The second thing I wanted to say was, um, in terms
of the history of this agreement, it has a long origin.
Uh, and from my side, as an official, one
of the positive things has been the strong bipartisan
support for this initiative.
Uh, the, the origins, of course,
for this were something called the Friends
of Fossil Fuel Subsidy Reform Group.
Uh, this was established in 2010.
Uh, the original membership was just New Zealand and Sweden.
Uh, in 2000
and, um, 15, um, prime Minister Key launched the Friends
of fossil fuel subsidy, communique at the, um,
at the cop at the Climate Change Conference.
And at the same time, in 2017 through until 2024, uh,
we launched a joint ministerial statement at the World
Trade Organization as well.
So we looked to do climate change,
and we looked to do the trade piece together.
And the work in the World Trade Organization has expanded
from 12 members that worked on it way back in 2017.
It expanded to 47, uh, this year, uh, in, uh,
2024 in Abu Dhabi.
So gradual incremental progress of, of support.
This negotiation itself was launched in September, 2019.
Uh, it was concluded in July, 2024.
Uh, and the signature was in the,
was virtual signature appropriately
for an agreement on climate change, no travel, and so on.
Um, mm-hmm. So it was signed
virtually on the 15th of November.
So just a few, uh, short weeks ago.
It will enter into force when three of the four of us, uh,
the four being New Zealand, uh, Iceland, Switzerland,
and Costa Rica, when three of us have brought the agreement,
uh, through our parliamentary processes and ratified it.
That then brings me to this
concept of open, plural lateralism.
So this is a, an agreement
that's deliberately designed not to be a closed agreement.
It's supposed to expand over time.
And we've got a long history of working
as New Zealand on these kinds of agreements.
We didn't use to call 'em open PLU lateral
agreements, but that is what they are.
The classical, uh,
and perhaps the most famous example is the P four agreement.
Uh, that was Singapore, New Zealand, Bruni and Chile.
I can remember, uh, appearing, uh, alongside in the way,
sorry, I should have introduced my colleague,
Rebecca Stephen Smith, who runs the unit that is, um,
implementing, uh, this important agreement.
I was in Rebecca's, uh, position, uh,
many years ago when we presented the P four agreement
to this committee.
Uh, and it was pointed out that it had cost more
to negotiate the agreement than the benefits of P four.
Uh, and I can remember David Walker,
who was the chief negotiator at the time, explaining
what the vision was, that over time this would expand.
There was plenty of skepticism, it's fair to say.
Um, and a sort
of a real questioning about what this approach was.
Well, P four, uh, is a really good example of
what we did signed in 2005.
Uh, it then emerged into, um, the TPP,
the Transpacific Partnership Agreement in 2015.
So 12 countries from four
to 12 brought in the United States, brought in Japan,
Canada, Mexico, and others.
It then became C-P-T-V-P.
Sadly, the United States withdrew, uh, president Trump.
Uh, and that agreement, CPTP, really important to us, uh,
has, was entered into force, uh, in 2018.
So it took 12 years for us
to move from this very small agreement with a number
of small countries, but a high quality, uh, agreement
and the commitment that numerous governments, uh, show
to this agreement over time, eventually emerged into, um,
what we have today in terms of the CPTP.
Another example of the, this approach
that we've adopted is the
Digitally Economy Partnership Agreement.
Again, three small countries, Singapore
and New Zealand, Chile devising rules, uh, trying
to make sure that the rules would work
for us in a digital space,
and then making it open to anyone
who could meet the standards, uh, of the agreement.
This one has worked much more quickly than, um, uh,
the P four because within a year of the agreement,
we got an application from a G 20 country, uh, Korea,
which is now a full member.
The group of countries looking
to join the Digital Economy Punch Agreement is now six,
includes China, Canada, so G seven Economy, El Salvador,
Ukraine, uh, United Arab Emirates, and Peru.
Global Trade in general Agreement is another good example.
Just three original members, New Zealand, Chile, and Canada.
Uh, it's now got 10 members.
So this idea of open, plural, lateralism really does work.
And the agreement on climate change, trade
and sustainability is, uh, exactly about, uh,
an agreement that we've created the four of us,
but that we want others to join
because that is where we're going
to have the most value from it.
Mm-hmm. So to turn to the three elements
of the agreement itself, the first is, um,
on environmental goods and services.
This is, as I said at the start,
this is the classical trade response to a climate problem.
You lower barriers, uh, you therefore lower the price, and
therefore you hope that your consumers
and producers will use those environmental technologies
and improve environmental outcomes, save money, uh,
but also, um, uh, help improve, uh, the environment.
But it is the classical trade response.
The same on environmental services.
You make commitments on environmental services
and services related to environmental protection.
And again, you lower the price of these
and you hope that people will take them up
because they are now cheaper, uh, than other, um, uh,
elements, uh, that you might use to manage this.
So, environmental goods, um, it seems like an obvious thing.
Um, but actually we have not been
successful internationally.
We have not been successful at the World Trade Organization
either to get a list of environmental goods, even to agree
how to define an environmental good.
The only example that we have out there is the 54 items
established in 2012 by apec, uh,
where there was an agreement on 54 items.
Uh, what we have,
or what you have in front of you today in,
in your examination processes 316 items
at the six digit level.
So, in fact, there are many more,
but it's 316 at, at that heading.
Things like solar panels, wind turbines,
air quality monitors, installation material, and so on.
So, classical elements of how you'd try to respond
to some environmental challenges out there,
perhaps significantly too.
What we've also done in this is we haven't just said, here's
what we agree as an environmental good.
We've actually explained why we think it's environmental
good, and that's the first time that's actually been
done internationally.
Importantly, too, uh, for New Zealand, uh, we have
for the first time included
and had defined
as an environmental good wood products and wool.
So two very significant items for us.
Uh, I was part of the Environmental Goods Agreement, uh,
process in apec,
and also a failed attempt at the World Trade Organization
to get an agreement and would,
and wool proved to be the most challenging items for some
of our colleagues to agree.
So what this agreement now does, it says wood
and wool are agreed, uh, as an environmental good.
And that is a really important advantage for us
as we look out to expand this agreement into the future.
Perhaps the third element, uh,
of the environmental goods piece is that, uh,
and this is part of the dynamic piece of this,
this is a, a living list.
So this list of 316 items can be regularly updated.
Uh, and the idea is that we will keep up
with emerging, uh, technology.
So to give you a really good example of something
that is not covered in the agreement,
because the harmonized system,
the customs tariff system has not been able
to keep up, is green hydrogen.
So we are not able to include that
because the, the system itself is not established in terms
of what the tariff would look like.
So the living list becomes very important if you think
about, uh, green hydrogen.
But also, if you think about all the critical minerals
that we need for the energy transition that's ahead of us,
getting them on this living list, uh,
is a really important dimension.
So the four of us certainly look
to make sure this is a dynamic process.
Environmental services, um, again,
little bit like environmental goods.
We've never had a proper definition
or a legally binding definition of
what an environmental service is.
So this gives us, for the first time an
expansive definition.
And it's really important because, uh,
the environmental services that we classically have thought
of were established in 9 95.
The environment and the challenges we face,
and the technologies and the way in which we service those,
and the way in which we deliver them,
including in the services space,
have significantly changed since 9 95.
So what's called the CPC Code system, uh,
a UN classification system that we use to schedule services
is essentially, well out of date.
What this agreement does it for the first time establishes
what is an environmental service.
And it associates that with not just an environmental good,
but also things like carbon markets.
So things we could not have envisaged at the end
of the Uruguay round in 9 95.
We are now picking up through this process.
And I should note that part of the origin
of the environmental services list
that we've agreed here comes out of apec, uh, out
of the APEC host year, uh, that New Zealand led, uh,
on acknowledge, um, honorable O'Connor, um, for, uh,
the work that was done through the Trade Minister's
dialogue, which helped establish, uh, those services.
That was an agreed list, uh, through apec.
Um, maybe now to turn to the fossil fuel subsidies chapter.
Uh, which in many ways was the most challenging
part of the negotiation.
Just to contextualize what we're trying to do here.
So the sums being spent internationally,
$1.6 trillion 2022.
So the OECD tells us $1.6 trillion
of taxpayer money spent on fossil fuel subsidization
for the first time in 2008, 2009, G 20 liter APEC leaders,
uh, and G seven leaders said,
we wanna reform fossil fuel subsidies.
Back then, less than half a trillion dollars was being spent
on fossil fuel subsidies.
Every year since then, APEC leaders, G 20 liters,
G seven liters, have been saying, we wanna reform.
There has been no reform.
And actually there's been an escalation
of fossil fuel subsidization.
So from less than half a trillion, we're now $1.6 trillion.
The elimination, uh,
of fossil fuel subsidies would be according to the IPCC.
If you remove fossil fuel subsidies,
that would be the equivalent of a 10% cut for the, uh, level
that we need to get to, uh, one point, uh, 5% by 2030.
To put that in context, we actually need a 43%
to achieve the 1.5.
So 10% is a very significant component of that.
The eus Joint Research Committee, which is their, uh,
joint Research Commission, which is their premier science
and innovation, uh, grouping estimates, uh, last year,
that if we could eliminate phos fuel subsidies,
that's the equivalent of 165 countries stopping their
elimination, uh, their emissions immediately.
So this is a very significant, uh,
thing that we're trying to do.
And of course, it only works if other countries then make
good on the commitment they make at these summits
and say, right, how are we gonna reform that?
What we're trying to say in this agreement is,
the way we have meaningfully reformed subsidies
internationally on other subjects like
agriculture, is through trade rules.
It is through trade rules,
because they're legally enforceable
that we actually make real difference.
So the friends of fossil fuel subsidies was designed
to lobby the G 20 leaders, the G seven leaders
and APEC leaders, to say, how are you gonna do this reform?
And we, as a small group of countries, a group
of small countries wanted to help 'em in that reform.
'cause of course, we are not actually, the significant
problem here out of
that has emerged the agreement on
climate change, trade and sustainability.
Essentially, we said in the end, we can't wait around
for the big guys anymore.
We are gonna design an agreement that is going
to address these subsidies.
And then we're gonna ask the G 20, the G seven,
and the APEC leaders, you've been saying you wanna do
reform, tell us why you can't be part of this agreement,
which actually would make it legally enforceable.
And so that is the, the genesis
of this chapter in this agreement.
Now, the elements of the agreement itself, uh,
it's the first time we've got a legally, uh,
binding in a trade agreement definition of
what is a fossil subsidy.
That's very significant indeed.
'cause it gives us a real meaningful starting point.
The reference point for that work is of course,
the WTO agreement on subsidies and Counter Valley measures.
And that is the reference point, which we've now expanded
to make sure that you could take into account not just the
trade effects, but the environmental effects as well.
That, again, is groundbreaking.
In the, in the agreement, we have three types of subsidies.
One is production of fossil fuels, the consumption
of fossil fuels, and, um, the production
of fossil fuel energy products.
So those three areas are, uh,
the areas of focus for the chapter.
Uh, three big things that we did.
The first is we've prohibited, in other words,
stopped subsidies to coal
and other fossil fuels with comparable emissions.
The second is, there's a standstill commitment by all of us,
and any new member would also be obliged to do that,
no new subsidies to the fossil fuel, uh, sector.
And the third is that you've gotta schedule any subsidy.
So it is the case that all of us, uh,
in this agreement do have some subsidies
to the fossil fuel industry.
Uh, we had to schedule those.
There has to be some transparency around those,
and those are subject to periodic reviews.
Now, at the same time, of course, there are exceptions, uh,
that we've agreed,
and there are essentially eight
exceptions to this agreement.
You can continue to use them if subsidies,
if you are using them to decommission, uh,
and get out of the fossil fuels things.
You can use subsidization to assist.
Uh, you can use subsidies if they are explicitly designed
to reduce greenhouse gas emissions
through your transition period.
The third one is you can support low income,
vulnerable populations.
Uh, and this is a very important dimension for the Pacific,
who of course, do subsidize fossil fuel, uh, use in
to move food and other products in
between the islands themselves.
So that is a recognition of that.
There has to be some transparency
around the scheduling of that.
But nevertheless, it's a really important, uh, element
that recognizes this is not a straightforward, uh, piece
of reform that people do.
Sorry, rather than running through the rest
of them, I'm just conscious of time.
Um, we might jump to the questions if, if you
Have, can I do the eco labeling one?
Sure. Just very quickly, and then, and I really am done.
So on eco labeling, that's the third pillar of the, uh,
agreement I mentioned in, uh, my presentation to the, uh,
committee, I think last week.
Um, one of the challenges that we face internationally is
that the way in which, uh, the rise
of protectionism is happening is also using environmental,
uh, reasons to try to block access.
So the WTOs estimated that in 2018,
there were less than a thousand new environment measures
that affected trade in 2023, that increased
to 3,200 new measures that affected trade that were driven
by an environmental reason.
So what this, uh, chapter does, it sets out some guidelines
about truthfulness, about making sure the consumer does have
the right to know about what the product that they have.
It's also designed to test the environmental
effectiveness of the products.
Those guidelines, we are all committed to, um, uh,
to promote and to share,
particularly with the private sector.
So one of the emerging challenges that I talked about, uh,
last week is the monopsony effects of supermarkets.
So the ability of these buyers of products, uh,
to prevent access to supermarket shelves
unless you are able to meet certain specific standards.
And I use the example of Stonewall maintenance programs
as an example of a a one size fits all
that works perfectly in the particular market that did that,
but has no relevance in the New Zealand context.
What the eco labeling chapter does is it provides some
measure of discipline that governments are obliged
to promote the guidelines,
but also are obliged to respond to questions.
If we see that in Iceland or Switzerland
or Costa Rica, there is a new private company
that's put into place a label
that we think is discriminatory or is unfair, uh,
or has unclear environmental benefits, we are able to ask
that government, that government then asks, um, the, uh,
the private sector firm,
and then there's a reporting process.
It goes to the point that I also tried
to make last week about the, the Dracula effect.
So getting transparency, getting sunlight on these things,
and hopefully that's the way in which you deal with, uh,
reducing that form of protectionism, because
That's the only mechanism, isn't it?
Sorry, The voluntary system though, isn't it?
And so the sunlight aspect will be the only way of trying to
It is, but I would argue that if you are a,
a serious country and you are participating in an agreement
like this, that you would take that responsibility serious
that this is part of international treaty. Yeah.
Great. Thank you. Um,
I'm just interested if you could give us some insight
around, um, the likelihood of
new entrance, what signals you might have
seen in that regard.
It's great to see some ambitious, um,
aspects of the agreement.
Uh, but I guess the question then is, if,
if they're too ambitious, is that potentially putting off
countries that might have signed up
to something slightly less ambitious?
Uh, and would some progress be better than total progress,
as it were in the sense of this, um, specifically
with relation to Fiji
and Norway, who are both initially involved
but have not signed up to this?
I'm interested in why that is.
So yes, it is a fine balance strike between ambition
that makes a difference and, uh, reflects some
of the actual realities on the ground.
So part of it was some of those exceptions that I was, uh,
walking through to try to recognize those conditions.
And that was, you know, certainly one of the areas
for Pacific Island countries is needing to know
that they would be able to continue that support.
Uh, but that they would have to also
be transparent about it.
I think the other element, um, is
that in the environmental goods, um, chapter,
there are a number of exceptions.
You can take a holiday, quote unquote, on certain forms
of certain tariff eliminations.
So you need to commit to the list,
but you can then, uh, prevent and limit some of those.
I mean, I'd have a question as to why you'd wanna do that,
but anyway, there are good reasons why people might want
to hold a tariff, whether it's protected domestic industry
or indeed, um, because of over capacity, uh, questions.
But there, so there are elements in each of the chapters
that are designed to respond to the kind of the reality
of trying to bring, um, countries in.
Um, yes, Fiji had been a participant,
but, um, moved out of it
because it has limited negotiating resources
to the explanation we were given.
Um, and they focused on the Indo-Pacific Economic Framework
Agreement, uh, which was being negotiated in, in parallel.
Uh, the other element, um, so we,
we are obviously interested in talking to the G seven
and the G 20 countries who
annually say they wanna make this reform.
Uh, and we wanna ask them,
can you tell us why you can't be part of this?
Yeah. Um, so, and,
and then we want to have a discussion about them.
You know, what, what is holding you back?
Uh, what is it that we've missed in this agreement
that you would need, uh, in there?
So you think about the uk, the eu, Canada, Korea,
uh, and others like that.
Then of course, you've got developing countries,
a whole suite of them, uh, Columbia, Ecuador,
the African countries, the Pacific Island states.
There is a, a a, a distinct difference
between the way in which they would all view this agreement.
Some will have to make commitments that will really
require them to make change, stop subsidization,
for example, eliminate tariffs.
Um, but for others, uh, the power will be,
here's a trade agreement that helps us contribute
to dealing with climate change.
Good. Uh,
Dr. Lawrence, Nan. Oh,
Um, Thank you, Mr. Chair.
You've touched on this sort of briefly, particularly
around the, I guess, the goal
around the fossil fuel subsidy,
but I guess my question is kinda overall,
what is the binding nature of this agreement?
So this is a binding agreement, so it has the possibility
of applying trade sanctions if someone, uh, renes on
or does not fulfill the commitments
and obligations in the agreement.
So it is a, in that sense, it's a classical trade agreement.
Okay.
Honorable Damien, Look, thank you very much.
And I, I think the complexity of this kind of, um,
gives weight to the argument that probably an oral,
um, briefing is, is useful.
And hopefully we could keep updating this.
Uh, just on one technical issue, we, um, we're going
to renew, the government's gonna renew, um, uh, drilling
for gas, and there has been a request by the industry
to guarantee that.
Would that be considered a subsidy under the fossil fuel
subsidies arrangements internationally?
So, so we would need to see the, the specifics of this,
but if it's part of the transition piece,
then certainly there's a carefully drafted exception
that would manage, um, that possibility.
So again, we, we'd be wanting to work with,
and we already do, uh, as part of the inter-agency process
with MB in particular, um, on how this, uh,
these, these will be managed.
We, we are confident that there are, to go
to your point about the re the real world dimension of this,
um, that there are practical ways to manage that.
Um, but it's not a, it's not a blank check.
Were there any key environmental goods from the
New Zealand perspective that didn't get across the line?
So from, I know, I would say that, uh, no.
Um, look, our big priority was a, a credible list so
that when people looked at it, uh, which is why the,
the descriptions of each product
and why it's an environment
of good becomes really important,
and that's actually codified in the agreement itself.
So for us, it was about the credibility of the list,
that people didn't look at it and think, why is that there?
Uh, that clearly responds to someone's commercial interest.
It's not an environmental product.
So that's one element, the credibility.
And the second was we do firmly believe that wool, uh,
as insulation as a way of managing oil spills, uh,
and wood, uh, sustainable products, uh,
that make a difference, uh, on the environment.
And that has been a, a strong position
of the New Zealand government of both sides of,
uh, of parliament on that.
So I don't believe that we missed anything
unless you could think of anything off the top of your
Head. Uh, the only
thing I would say is,
and some of the codes are a wee bit old, so I think we
registered that there were, were things that if the,
when the codes are updated, we'd like to see that list
Such as Green, green, hydrogen hydrogen's,
the one we were referring to.
Yeah. So green hydrogen is definitely one,
and then there's the critical minerals piece.
Mm-hmm. Uh, so as that, as our understanding of the role
that critical minerals play in the energy transition.
Uh, so a lot of those critical minerals are vital
for hybrid batteries and so on,
and the way in which that helps in the transition,
picking those up in the, uh,
in the agreement will be really important.
That's why the living list concept
that New Zealand pioneered in the World Trade Organization
and brought into this agreement is so important.
That's, that's how you keep this agreement relevant in a
normal, sorry, quote unquote, a normal trade agreement.
You'd have a review period between five and seven years.
That's what you normally do. This living list thing
is a really dynamic one. Mm-hmm.
Good. Daniel Kirkpatrick,
Uh, thank you. Um,
I was just interested to understand a, if any, uh,
disadvantages to New Zealand, uh, from this agreement,
because we should always, you know, think about that
as well, um, particularly in cost wise or in, um, in trade.
So it, it, it's a good question.
Um, yes, it depends a little bit on your perspective.
Um, so yes, some would argue that, uh, one
of the disadvantages that we eliminate this tariff,
the tariffs on environment of goods to everybody, uh,
and that we therefore lose a modest amount of revenue, uh,
from the, from the tariffs themselves.
Of course, that's offset by the benefit
to New Zealand producers
of having cheaper product, uh, in New Zealand.
And also I'd say that that's also consistent
with international rules, be international trade rules,
because it is not possible to do an agreement like this, um,
and keep it in accordance with, uh, wt O rules
unless you make the commitment to all partners, uh,
that you're gonna share the benefits of the agreement
because there's something called, um,
substantially all trade.
So for a trade agreement to be consistent
with the WTO O rules, it needs
to cover substantially all trade.
If it does not, and this does not cover substantially all
trade, you know, it doesn't include
manufacture all manufactured products.
It doesn't include all agricultural products, for example.
So because of that, the four of us who are big supporters
of the rules-based trading system said,
we're gonna MFN that commitment.
In other words, we're gonna
make that commitment to everybody.
Mm-hmm. Uh, and of course, I would argue, um,
and in any case, most of our tariffs are at zero
or uh, less than five.
There are some exceptions.
Um, but again, the benefit of the removal in these cases,
in terms of the environmental, uh, benefit
and the uptake, uh, are significant.
And I guess also there'd be, um, the argument that, um,
binding ourselves in on fossil fuel subsidies might, uh,
might cause some people some anxiety.
Again, it's about transparency, it's about scheduling.
And I would say that we're also part
of the APEC leaders' commitment, uh, that it's been saying
for many years that we need to, to do this.
And again, that disadvantage versus the advantage
that New Zealand exporters are going to get,
because you start to level that playing field
and the better return
that New Zealand exporters are gonna get once others
lose their subsidies.
And the competitiveness, the positive competitiveness
effects for us, I think significantly outweigh the, um,
the perceived disadvantage. Great. Thank you.
What's your estimate of the timeframe it would take for
that cost benefit to be in our favor?
So, I, I would say this,
but I, I believe already that benefit is, uh, in our favor
because we've got this new access
to at least three partners.
Switzerland is a not insignificant,
uh, partner in this space.

source_metadata.description:
Thursday 5 December 2024 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee

International treaty examination of the Agreement on Climate Change, Trade and Sustainability; Briefing on non-tariff barriers

International treaty examination of the Agreement on Climate Change

Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade:
- Vangelis Vitalis, Deputy Secretary
- Rebecca Steffens-Smith, Chief Negotiator

Briefing on non-tariff barriers

Te Taumata
- Hone McGregor, Chair
